coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots boosted resistive switching and magnetic properties in Co3O4-based memory devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.prism:issueName: The 13th Asian Meeting on Ferroelectrics jointly with the 13th Asian Meeting on Electroceramics
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Chuangye
Wu, Qixiao
Tian, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could create opportunities of modulating resistive switching behaviors due to their unique electrical performance. Here, CQDs are introduced to enhance switching uniformity and magnetic properties in Co3O4 thin film memory devices. Compared with the pure Co3O4-based device, the device with CQDs exhibited better performance, such as a reduced initialization voltage, uniform distribution of Set and Reset voltages, stable endurance behavior, and enhanced magnetization performance. The fitting results of current-voltage curves showed that the low-resistance states and high-resistance states of the devices obey Ohmic conduction and Schottky emission, respectively. The improvement in resistive switching properties is ascribed to the formation and rupture of confined conductive filaments due to that CQDs enhanced the local electric field, which can efficiently guide the filament growth and suppress the randomness of filament growth. Magnetization modulation associated with resistive switching involves in the variation of oxygen vacancies concentration and the conversion between Co2+ and Co3+. The present study exhibits an effective path to enhance resistive switching and magnetic properties, which shows promising application to design multifunctional electro-magnetic integrated devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Co3O4 thin films
Resistive switching
Conductive filaments
Magnetization enhancement

X2023050 and X2023054 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing the fabrication process for the device with Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt structure.

(a) XRD patterns of Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt stacked structure. (b) XPS analysis of high-resolution C 1s spectrum of the CQDs grown on the surface of bottom (Pt) electrode. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) Co 2p and (d) O 1s from Co3O4 thin films deposited on CQDs-decorated Pt substrates.

(a) TEM image of CQDs. (b) The magnified TEM image illustrating a selected region (red circle) for further analysis, inset figures showing the optical images of CQDs under A: visible light and B: 365 nm UV light illumination. (c) The corresponding HRTEM image from the selected region. (d) Histogram of size distribution of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) AFM image of Co3O4 thin films. (b) The corresponding three-dimensional surface of Co3O4 thin films. (c) AFM image of CQDs onto Pt substrates. (c) Side-view SEM image of Co3O4 thin films.

(a) Initial current-voltage characteristics (Forming process) of Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices. (b) Statistical distribution of the Forming voltage of Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices from different 100 cells. Typical current-voltage curves after Forming process of (c) Pt/Co3O4/Pt and (e) Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices. Histogram showing the corresponding distribution of the Reset and Set voltages from (d) Pt/Co3O4/Pt and (f) Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices.
(a) Endurance performance comparison of Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices. (b) Retention capability of the HRS and LRS of Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt device.

(a) Typical double-log scale current-voltage fitting for resistive switching of Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt device. (b) The ln (I) versus V1/2 plot of HRS. (c) Temperature dependence test of HRS and LRS of Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt device.

(a) M−H loops of Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt devices at room temperature. (b) The corresponding M−H loops at low magnetic field. (c) M−H loops of Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt device for the IS (initial state), HRS, and LRS.
Schematic diagrams illustrating resistive switching mechanism of Co3O4-based device with CQDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots boosted resistive switching and magnetic properties in Co3O4-based memory devices

School of Chemistry and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Xiangnan Rare-Precious Metals Compounds Research and Application, School of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Xiangnan University, Chenzhou 423000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could create opportunities of modulating resistive switching behaviors due to their unique electrical performance. Here, CQDs are introduced to enhance switching uniformity and magnetic properties in Co3O4 thin film memory devices. Compared with the pure Co3O4-based device, the device with CQDs exhibited better performance, such as a reduced initialization voltage, uniform distribution of Set and Reset voltages, stable endurance behavior, and enhanced magnetization performance. The fitting results of current-voltage curves showed that the low-resistance states and high-resistance states of the devices obey Ohmic conduction and Schottky emission, respectively. The improvement in resistive switching properties is ascribed to the formation and rupture of confined conductive filaments due to that CQDs enhanced the local electric field, which can efficiently guide the filament growth and suppress the randomness of filament growth. Magnetization modulation associated with resistive switching involves in the variation of oxygen vacancies concentration and the conversion between Co2+ and Co3+. The present study exhibits an effective path to enhance resistive switching and magnetic properties, which shows promising application to design multifunctional electro-magnetic integrated devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advance of the big data era and informative industry, the traditional flash memory technique has been faced with the impediment of physical scaling limitations and von Neumann architecture owing to that Moore's law is coming [1,2]. In this case, the emerging information-storage memories have captured considerable attention, and the resistive switching memory devices with two-terminal structure exhibited a huge potential for next-generation nonvolatile data storage applications such as excellent scalability, good compatibility, and high storage capacity [3].

However, in a commercial perspective, current resistive random access memory (RRAM) devices need to solve severe variability issue of resistive switching parameters [4]. The extreme variation of switching voltages in oxide-based RRAM is one of the major obstacles preventing the commercial applications of RRAM, which may lead to false programming and readout hazards [4–6]. Therefore, it is important and meaningful for oxide-based RRAM to acquire the uniformity of switching voltages. Usually, the switching voltages of RRAM exhibited a broad variation because of the large stochasticity in the growth and fracture of conductive filaments [4,7,8], which caused principally by the lack of spatial and geometrical control of the conductive filamentary paths which play critical roles in the resistive switching behaviors. Such facts stimulated researchers to develop novel device architecture for formation of confined filament growth, including bilayer structure [9], construction of pyramid electrode [10–12], impurity doping using metal nanoparticles [13,14], and interface engineering etc [15,16]. In these methods, the bilayer structure technique is lack of universal material types, limited to specific material combination. The construction of pyramid electrode is high manufacturing requirements and complex manufacturing process [10,11]. Impurity doping using metal nanoparticles is a favorable method to control the conductive filamentary paths. Hao et al. prepared Au-doped NiFe2O4-based memory device, and reported that the location of formation and fracture of conductive filament is modulated near Au nanoparticles [13]. Nevertheless, it is difficult to determine the exact sites of filament disruption and growth because of the random distribution of doped nanoparticles within resistive switching layer. Interface engineering is a feasible approach to exactly control the location of filament formation and disruption on the interface between electrode and switching layer, by inserting small particle on the electrode surface (i. e. nanocrystals and quantum dots), which is compatible with large-scale implementation to achieve good uniformity and stability of resistive switching parameters [6]. Conductive filaments prior to create near small particle due to the enhancement of local electric field induced by high curvature radius, and the location of rupture and regeneration of filaments is controlled near small particle [17]. It is feasible that introduction of quantum dots to memristors can improve resistive switching performance [6]. Yan et al. introduced PbS quantum dots to Ga2O3-based memory devices and reported uniformity improvement of switching voltages due to the enhancement of local electric field [6]. Bera et al. adopted CdSe quantum dots to fabricate Al/CdSe-PVP/Al structure devices, and reported ultralow-power consumption [18]. Furthermore, Cao et al. prepared lead-free Cs3Bi2Br9 perovskite quantum dots for eco-friendly RRAM applications [19]. In contrast, CQDs have a huge potential for modulation of resistive switching properties because of their high conductivity, eco-friendly characteristics, simple component, low-cost preparation, and stable physical or chemical properties [20]. Ding et al. found that graphite microislands (GMs) grown on bottom electrode can enhance resistive switching characteristics due to the enhancement of local electric field [21]. Thus, it is speculated that introducing CQDs to decorate electrode interface may improve the switching parameters of RRAM devices. By introducing CQDs to oxide-based memory devices, conductive filaments tend to create near CQDs because of the enhancement of local electric field, and the location of filament rupture occurs near CQDs, further improving switching uniformity [17,21].

To date, many oxide-based materials have been reported as switching layer of RRAM device. In particular, spinel oxide-based materials found excellent resistive switching performance such as spinel-like MgZnO [22], magnetic ferrites (e.g., CoFe2O4, NiFe2O4, ZnFe2O4) [23–25], and binary cobalt oxide (Co3O4) [26]. Among these spinel materials, spinel Co3O4 is an outstanding candidate for filament-based RRAM applications in view of the advantages of easy migration ability of oxygen ions, friendly compatibility with magnetization, and controllable valence variation of Co2+/Co3+ via electric field regulation [27,28], which is desirable for design of high-performance RRAM devices. In addition, the resistive switching induced change of room temperature ferromagnetism in RRAM devices provide potential to create four logical states for high-density information storage and multifunctional electromagnetism memory device applications [24,29], which motivated us to clarify the relevancy between resistive switching and magnetization modulation, further unearthing physical switching mechanism.

Herein, we applied the interface engineering strategy to design a memory device that can achieve uniform switching parameters by utilizing CQDs, due to their unique electronic properties. Spinel Co3O4 thin film for switching layer and CQDs for interface modification were prepared by chemical solution deposition and ball mill method, respectively. The impact of inserting CQDs on the resisitive and magnetic switching properties was explored. CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device exhibited smaller operating voltage, narrower voltage distribution, and improved uniformity and stability as compared to the pure Co3O4-based device. The relevant physical mechanisms of resistance switching and magnetism modulation were proposed based on the analysis of temperature dependent resistance, current conduction mechanism, and magnetic switching characteristics.

To inspect the impact of interface modification with CQDs on the resistive switching parameters and magnetization modulation properties, spinel Co3O4-based memory devices with/without CQDs were fabricated for comparative study (Pt/Co3O4/Pt and Pt/Co3O4/CQDs/Pt). The pure Co3O4-based device without CQDs was prepared using a low-cost chemical solution deposition method as reported in our previous research [27]. The schematic diagram for fabricating CQDs/Co3O4 composited device is displayed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the solution of CQDs with narrow size distribution was prepared by a simple high-energy ball milling method. For a typical process, 5.0 g raw powders mixing activated carbon and potassium carbonate with the stoichiometric ratio of 2: 1 as precursor composition were milled in a stainless steel vial with stainless balls on a planetary ball mill (Fritsch P7). The milling speed and time were 300 rpm and 40 h, respectively, following a ball-to-powder weight ratio of 50:1. Then, the milled mixture was dispersed into 50 mL deionized water, obtaining a suspension with CQDs. The obtained suspension was centrifuged at a speed of 10 000 rpm for 10 min, yielding a transparent supernatant solution contained CQDs. Therefore, the desired CQDs solution was successfully achieved. In this study, CQDs solution of 1 mg/mL was used by suspending 10 mg CQDs into deionized water of 10 mL. Afterwards, in order to fabricate interface modification of CQDs, the resultant CQDs solution was spin-coated onto Pt/Ti/SiO2/Si substrates at a speed of 1000 rpm for 30 s, followed by vacuum drying treatment for 12 h, and the stacked structure of CQDs/Pt/Ti/SiO2/Si was obtained. Meanwhile, Co3O4 precursor solution of 0.1 mol/L was synthesized by mixing cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (solute) and co-solvents composed of ethylic acid and 2-methoxyethanol with volume ratio of 1: 2. After that the obtained precursor solution of Co3O4 was spin-coated on the surface of CQDs/Pt substrates at a speed of 3000 rpm for 30 s, next baking at 350 °C for 5 min. The process of spin-coating and baking were repeatedly operated till achieving the needed film thickness. To obtain compact and crystallized thin films, the films were thermally annealed at 600 °C for 1 h. Finally, a top electrode of platinum was deposited on the surface of Co3O4 films using direct-current (DC) sputtering method.

The structure of the pure Co3O4-based device and CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device was characterized using a X-ray diffractometer of Rigaku D/Max-2400 (XRD). The chemical information (i.e. valence state, element types) of CQDs and Co3O4 films was examined by means of ESCLab-250 X-ray photoeletron spectrum measurements (XPS). Transmission electron microscopy of FEI Tecnai (TEM) was employed to observe morphology characteristics of CQDs. A Bruker Dimension lcon atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to inspect the surface characteristics and particle size. The morphologies and thickness of thin film were obtained via scanning electron microscopy of Gemini 500 instrument (SEM). A Keithley 236 sourcemeter semiconductor analyzer was used to acquire electrical parameters of the devices such as current-voltage curves (I–V), endurance, and retention test. The temperature-dependent I–V characteristics were also analyzed from 25 to 85 °C. Magnetic hysteresis (M−H) loops of the devices were obtained using a superconducting quantum interference device magnetometer (SQUID) at room temperature. The tested films were placed parallel to the magnetic field with the range of −2 to 2 Tesla.

The XRD characterization was performed to examine the crystal structures of Co3O4-based devices with/without CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), XRD results revealed three distinctive peaks corresponding to (220), (511), and (440) crystal planes of spinel Co3O4 material from diffraction data (JCPDS no.: 74-2120) [30]. No diffraction peaks from impurities such as CoO were found, and there is no identification of the characteristic peaks from CQDs in the device with CQDs, due to the trace CQDs content transcending the detection limit (>5%) for XRD measurement [31]. The CQDs have been characterized following succedent analysis. Fig. 2(b) displays XPS analysis of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CQDs grown on the surface of bottom platinum electrode, and the XPS peaks at 288.3, 285.2, and 284.6 eV can be assigned to CO, C–O,and C–C, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, the surface of Co3O4 thin films deposited on CQDs-decorated bottom electrode was analyzed using XPS technology, as shown in Fig. 2(c and d). The high-resolution Co 2p spectrum from Fig. 2(c) shows a pair of satellite and two sharp peaks (2p1/2 and 2p3/2), suggesting the coexistence of Co (II) and Co (III) in the Co3O4 thin films [26]. Based on the analysis of the fitting curve of narrow-scan Co 2p region, the characteristic peaks at 781.7 eV and 796.3 eV belong to Co (II) species, and the binding energy peaks at 779.8 eV and 794.7 eV can be attributed to Co (III) species [33]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(d) is resolved into two peaks. The fitted peak at 530.0 eV is assigned to lattice oxygen, and the higher peak at 531.5 eV corresponds to oxygen vacancies which is desirable in Co3O4-based memory device for triggering resistive switching effect [34].

Fig. 3 shows TEM characterization to acquire detailed analysis of the prepared CQDs. Fig. 3(a) displays a typical TEM image, which was magnified for further observation, shown in Fig. 3(b). As observed that carbon dots represent quasi-spherical shape and dispersed distribution. Inset of Fig. 3(b) shows the optical images of the CQDs suspension under visible light (A) and its blue fluorescence following 365 nm UV irradiation. In TEM image for Fig. 3(b), a selected region (marked with red circle) is analyzed by means of HRTEM characterization, as shown in Fig. 3(c). It is found that from Fig. 3(c) the HRTEM image of the selected region depict interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of (100) plane of CQDs [35]. Fig. 3(d) shows the statistical size distribution through the Gauss distribution fitting [27], in which the average diameter of the CQDs is approximately 3.2 nm in size.

To further analyze the surface and interface morphologies of Co3O4-based device decorated with CQDs, AFM image of Co3O4 thin films was recorded, as shown in Fig. 4 (a and b). The corresponding root-mean-square roughness (RMS) and average roughness were 3.59 and 2.81, respectively, which revealed that the solution-derived Co3O4 thin films have smooth surface. According to AFM characterization, the size of surface particle is around 35 nm by the profile confirmation. Fig. 4(c) shows the AFM image of CQDs grown at bottom electrode. It is clearly found that CQDs achieved a cellular arrangement with separated spacing. The RMS and average roughness were 1.33 and 0.99, respectively. Fig. 4(d) displays the side-view SEM image of Co3O4 thin films deposited on CQDs-decorated platinum substrates, in which the thickness of Co3O4 thin films is approximately 100 nm.

For measuring resistive switching properties, the voltage was applied on the top electrode with the bottom electrode grounded. Firstly, the devices suffer from a Forming process to initate the memory devices to a low resistance state (LRS), imposed a compliance current with 10 mA to avoid cell breakdown due to Joule overheating, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). As observed that the pure Co3O4-based device without CQDs possesses a higher switching voltage of 7.2 V at Forming process, while the device with CQDs only entails a smaller voltage of 3.8 V to realize Forming process, illustrating that interface modification of CQDs is benifical for inducing resistive switching effect. To further compare with the switching uniformity of Forming process, this work measured 100 cells to achieve statistics of the Forming voltage, as shown in Fig. 5(b). It is found that the change of the pure Co3O4-based device in Forming voltage is much greater than that of the device with CQDs. This indicates that interface modification of CQDs can improve the switching uniformity of Forming process, which may be attributed to CQDs serving as seeds for inducing growth of conducting filament [36]. Based on that the interface modification of CQDs between Co3O4 thin films and bottom electrode can create an enhancement of local electric field, which efficiently regulates the filament growth such as morphology, shape, and orientation, further acting as seeds to induce resistive switching behaviors [17,21,36,37]. Ding et al. prepared graphite microislands (GMs) grown on bottom Ni electrode, and fabricated Cu/carbon/GMs/Ni stacked devices [21]. In their research, conductive filaments tend to connect toward the GMs on the surface of bottom Ni electrode and prefer to grow along GMs due to the enhancement of local electric field [21]. Shi et al. deposited Ag nanoparticles on the surface of bottom electrode and prepared ZnO-based devices decorated with Ag nanoparticles [36]. Compared with the pure ZnO-based devices without Ag particles, Ag particles-decorated ZnO-based devices acquired improvement in resistive switching performance, which is ascribed to that Ag particles act as seeds for growth of conductive filaments, resulting in depressed randomness of the conducting paths [36]. Subsequently, sweeping again from 0 V to −1.0 V following a negative bias voltage without compliance current, the device switches to a high resistance state (HRS), called Reset process.The device also showed the reversible switching from HRS to LRS following Set process, swept back to a positive bias voltage direction. Through measuring 100 consecutive switching, the typical current-voltage (I–V) curves of the pure Co3O4-based device and CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device were depicted in Fig. 5(c) and (e), respectively. All devices exhibited an obvious bipolar resistive switching characteristics. The corresponding switching voltage distributions of the pure Co3O4-based device and CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device were displayed in Fig. 5(d) and (f), respectively. The pure Co3O4-based device exhibited a broader voltage distribution with VReset from −0.7 to −0.54 V, while the narrower distribution was obtained in CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device such as Reset voltage (−0.58 to −0.5 V). In addition, the distribution of the pure Co3O4-based device in Set voltage (+0.75 to +1.7 V) is much greater than that of the device with CQDs from +0.95 to +1.3 V. It is important to note that interface modification of CQDs can significantly improve switching uniformity of resistive switching process.

To inspect the influence of CQDs decoration on the device stability, the endurance performance comparison of all devices was depicted in Fig. 6 (a). Compared with the device with CQDs, the pure Co3O4-based device manifested obvious fluctuation, suggesting that the CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device acquire reliable switching reproducibility for RRAM applications. Fig. 6(b) shows the time-dependent resistance at different resistance states of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device, which can be well maintained without any degradation over 104 s and verified stable nonvolatile characteristics. Moreover, the ratio between HRS and LRS (ROFF/RON) of >102 is found, which avoids false-readout risks and satisfy requirements of practical RRAM device applications [38].

The growth and disruption process of conducting filaments implies that different current conducting mechanism may be existed at LRS and HRS of the memory device. Fig. 7 (a) shows a typical double-logarithmic scale plot of I–V fitting for resistive switching to analyze the conduction mechanism of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device. The carrier transport behavior with a slope approaching 1 at LRS was corresponded to Ohmic conduction owing to filament formation. In the low-voltage region of HRS, the carrier transport behavior also obeys Ohmic conduction behavior due to that the content of the injected carrier is lower than that of the equilibrium carrier in resistive switching layer [26]. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the carrier transport characteristics at higher voltage region of HRS can be well fitted by the relationship of Ln (I) versus Sqrt (V), suggesting that Schottky emission mechanism is dominant and conducting filaments is disrupted [24]. To further reveal the physical nature of carrier conduction, Fig. 7(c) depicts the resistance-temperature relationship of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device at a read voltage of 0.1 V. The device exhibited a semiconducting behavior at HRS because of that the resistance value decreases with increasing temperature, whereas the resistance value at LRS increases via increasing temperature, illustrating a metal-like behavior and filament establishment at LRS of the device. In the LRS of the device with CQDs, the resistance temperature coefficient (α) was examined following the typical expression, that is R(T) = R0[1+α(T-T0)], and its value is obtained to be 3.8 × 10−3 K-1. According to literature, a similar coefficient (α = 1.2 × 10−3 K-1) was found in the Pt/Co3O4/MnO2/Pt memory devices, where conductive filaments were composed of oxygen vacancies (Co3O4 layer) and Mn atoms (MnO2 layer) [39]. In addition, Wang et al. fabricated Pt/NiFe2O4/Ag nanoparticles/NiFe2O4/Pt and Pt/NiFe2O4/Cu nanoparticles/NiFe2O4/Pt devices [40]. In their research, a resistance temperature coefficient of α = 2.7 × 10−3 K-1 at LRS of Pt/NiFe2O4/Ag nanoparticles/NiFe2O4/Pt devices was corresponded to conductive filaments composed of Ag and oxygen vacancies, and a resistance temperature coefficient of α = 1.4 × 10−3 K-1 at LRS of Pt/NiFe2O4/Cu nanoparticles/NiFe2O4/Pt devices was ascribed to conductive filaments composed of Cu and oxygen vacancies [40]. Accordingly, it may be proposed that carbon atoms from CQDs can also contribute to the formation of conductive filaments. Therefore, these results indicates that the repeatedly switching between HRS and LRS in CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device can be attributed to the formation and disruption of conductive filaments composed of oxygen vacancies (Co3O4 layer) and carbon atoms (CQDs layer).

After inspecting the interface modification effect of CQDs on the resistive switching performance, this effect on the magnetization modulation properties was also examined. Fig. 8 (a) shows the magnetic hysteresis (M−H) of the devices with/without CQDs, measured at room temperature. It is found that all devices present ferromagnetic behaviors although the bulk Co3O4 owns the intrinsic antiferromagnetic characteristics, the detailed M−H loops at low magnetic field were displayed in Fig. 8 (b). This phenomenon of room temperature ferromagnetism in the antiferromagnetic metal oxides is originated from oxygen vacancies introduced by size dependence of nanoparticles in spinel Co3O4 thin films [27,28], and the interface modification of CQDs enhanced saturation magnetization (Ms) from 34 to 49 emu/cc, which is desirable for creating ferromagnetism semiconductors for spintronics applications. The magnetism of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based devices is stronger than that of the pure Co3O4-based devices. Enhancement of ferromagnetic behavior following CQDs modulation can be ascribed to the increment of the ferromagnetic Co2+ due to that electron transfer from CQDs to Co3+ in Co3O4 [28]. For the M−H loops of Fig. 8(b), it is clearly found that the two devices exhibited magnetic exchange bias effect, which can be attributed to the interfaces magnetic interaction between antiferromagnetic layer (Pt) and ferromagnetic layer (i.e. Co3O4 or CQDs/Co3O4 nanocomposite thin films) [41]. The magnetization modulation induced by resistive switching is of vital importance for designing multifunctional electro-magnetic integrated devices. Fig. 8(c) shows M−H loops of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based devices for different resistance states: IS (the initial state without forming process), HRS, and LRS. As observed that the resistive switching modulated variation of saturation magnetization obeys the order of Ms (LRS) > Ms (HRS) > Ms (IS), suggested that resistive switching and magnetization modulation simultaneously were achieved by electric-field manipulation. The Ms values of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based devices at IS, HRS, and LRS are obtained to be 49, 95, and 113 emu/cc, respectively, which indicated that magnetization modulation of as high as 57% was realized. In our previous research, we found that the Ms variations of pure Co3O4-based devices also obey the order of Ms (LRS) > Ms (HRS) > Ms (IS) [27]. Therefore, the devices at LRS exhibited the most significant magnetic properties in regardless of the pure Co3O4-based devices or CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based devices. Resistive switching manipulated multilevel saturation magnetization in CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device can be ascribed to the change of oxygen vacancies concentration and cation valence state [27]. XPS results of Fig. 2 indicated that oxygen vacancies can be occurred in Co3O4 thin films at the initial state, which is beneficial for inducing ferromagnetic behavior [27,42]. Ferromagnetic behaviors of the Co3O4 thin films originate from the magnetic ions of Co2+ [28,43]. The value of Ms at the initial state of the device is lower than that of the HRS and LRS, which can be ascribed to low concentration of Co2+ and oxygen vacancies. For the HRS or LRS of the devices, suffering from electrical forming process, lots of oxygen vacancies are introduced into Co3O4 thin films by electrical impulses [24]. The created oxygen vacancies can induce nonmagnetic Co3+ converting into magnetic Co2+ due to redox effect [24,27], which causes increment of Co2+ and Ms at HRS and LRS of the device. Similarly, the device at LRS obtained higher concentration of oxygen vacancies and Co2+ as compered to HRS of the device. During the Reset process, the oxygen vacancies at HRS of the device decreases due to electric field effect, which induces the conversion of Co2+ to Co3+ owing to redox effect [24,27], further causing that the Ms (HRS) is lower as compared to the Ms value of LRS. The magnetic switching characteristics associated with resistive switching can give a clue for designing multifunctional electro-magnetic memory devices.

Based on the above analysis and discussion, we propose a model to describe the resistive switching mechanism of CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device, as shown in Fig. 9 . During the Forming or Set process, under a positive bias voltage, the fabricated memory devices can switch from HRS to LRS through growth of cone-shaped conductive filaments between top and bottom electrode [44]. During the Reset process, under a negative bias voltage, LRS of the devices can switch to HRS owing to filament disruption caused by current-induced Joule heat effect [45]. Subsequently, the positive Set process makes the conducting filaments recover again by means of the electric field effect. Therefore, the reversible process of filament formation and disruption via manipulating electric field realizes repetitive switching between HRS and LRS of the devices. Simultaneously, the change of oxygen vacancies concentration at different resistance states induces the conversion of cation valence state (Co2+ and Co3+) due to redox reaction, further realizing magnetic switching [27]. In general, the stochastic growth/disruption of conductive filaments caused dispersed resistive switching parameters [7,8]. By introducing CQDs to Co3O4-based devices, the resistive switching properties can be enhanced via the formation and disruption of confined conductive filaments composed of oxygen vacancies and carbon atoms, which can control the location of filament disruption and regeneration near CQDs [39]. For the CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based devices, CQDs can generate the enhancement of local electric field due to high curvature radius [17], and conductive filaments prior to create along local electric field, resulting in that the location of filament rupture/regeneration is confined near CQDs. Therefore, CQDs help to confine the location of filament formation/disruption, which results in the localization of switching sites, further confining filament growth/disruption along the same path. Hence, it can be deduced that switching sites are localized in Co3O4-based device with CQDs, due to formation and disruption of confined conductive filaments, which can suppress the randomness of filament growth/rupture and realize the improvement in uniformity and stability of resistive switching parameters.

Influence of interface modification with CQDs on the resistive switching and magnetization modulation properties of spinel Co3O4-based memory device was investigated. CQDs-decorated Co3O4-based device exhibited uniformity in voltage distribution such as Forming process and Set/Reset process, stability in endurance and retention performance, and multilevel magnetic switching. Improvement in uniformity and stability of resistive switching parameters is ascribed to the formation and disruption of confined conductive filaments due to the enhancement of local electric field, suppressing the randomness of filament growth. Resistive switching induced magnetization modulation is attributed to the changes of oxygen vacancies concentration and cation valence state (Co2+ and Co3+). Results suggested that the interface modification of CQDs provides an effective avenue to enhance resistive switching properties and design multifunctional electro-magnetic integrated devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

X2023050 and X2023054).
